coredata.dc:title: Carbon quantum dots@metal–organic framework based catalytic nucleic acid fluorescent system for highly sensitive and selective detection of Pb2+ in aqueous solutions
coredata.dc:creator: Jain, Shikha
Dilbaghi, Neeraj
Kumar Singhal, Nitin
Kaushik, Ajeet
Kim, Ki-Hyun
Kumar, Sandeep
coredata.dc:description: Accumulation of heavy metal ions in the ecosystem has become a major concern due to their adverse impacts on animals, humans, and aquatic habitats. The development of a rapid and portable sensing system is important for the prompt detection and proper management of such pollutants. Herein, we have designed a fluorescence-based biosensor for the selective and sensitive detection of Pb2+ ions using a DNAzyme system that is active only in the presence of lead (II) ions (Pb2+). The developed nanoprobe relies on two major components: (i) boron and nitrogen carbon dots (BNCDs)-doped carboxyl functionalized-terbium metal-organic framework (COOH-Tb MOF) as a fluorescent tag and (ii) quencher-modified catalytic NH3-GR5 DNAzyme as a bioreceptor molecule. The fluorescent tag (BNCDs/MOF) exhibits dual fluorescence with emission peaks located in the regions of blue and green. In the absence of Pb2+ ions, this hybridization undergoes reduced emission intensity because of the overlap in fluorescence emission between BNCDs@Tb-MOF and quenchers. However, as the catalytic core of the DNAzyme strand is activated in the presence of Pb2+ ions, the fluorescent emission of BNCDs/Tb-MOF resumes with the cleavage of quencher-tagged substrate strand. Here, the release of a shorter oligo sequence effectively increases the intensity of the proposed novel biosensor to realize low level detection of Pb2+ (e.g., to 0.96 ppb in a wide detection range from 2 to 1000 nM). The establishment of this novel biosensing approach is expected to offer new insights into rapid, selective, and sensitive detection of major pollutants for food and environmental safety.

Microscopic characteristics of Tb-MOF and its composites: (a-c) FESEM images, (d-f) HRTEM images, and (g-i) SAED patterns of Tb-MOF, BNCDs@Tb-MOF, and silanized-BNCDs@Tb-MOF, respectively.

Structural analysis of all synthesized materials: (a) FTIR analysis of the BNCDs (black), Tb-MOF (red) and BNCDs/Tb-MOF (blue) and BNCDs/Tb-MOF@DNAzyme (pink). (b) XRD analysis of the BNCDs (black), Tb-MOF (red), BNCDs/Tb-MOF (blue). (For interpretation of the references to colour in this figure legend, the reader is referred to the web version of this article.)

XPS spectrum of Tb-MOF: (a) survey spectrum, (b) Tb spectrum, (C) C1s spectra, and (D) O1s spectra.
XPS spectrum of BNCDs/Tb-MOF: (a) survey spectrum, (b) Tb spectra, (C) C1s spectra, and (D) B1s spectra.

Optical characteristics of BNCDs and composites (a, d, and g) in relation to UV–vis absorption spectra (Inset: dispersion of corresponding material with (right) and without (left) UV illumination), (b, e, and h) PL spectra, and (c, f, and i) optical patterns of BNCDs, Tb-MOF, BNCDs/Tb-MOF, respectively.

CIE 1931 color space chromaticity diagram in the (x, y) coordinates system with PL excitation at 250 nm: (a) blue color (black dot) emission from BNCDs, (b) green color (circle) emission from Tb-MOF, and (c) greenish blue color (circle) emission from BNCDs@Tb-MOF. (For interpretation of the references to colour in this figure legend, the reader is referred to the web version of this article.)

Characterization of BNCDs@Tb-MOF@DNAzyme nanoprobe: (a) gel images demonstrate the changes in mobility of BNCDs@Tb-MOF before and after conjugation with GR5 DNAzyme and (b) PL spectra of BNCDs@Tb-MOF before and after the conjugation with the GR5 DNAzyme.

Fluorescent sensing of Pb2+ ions: (a) gradual elevation in fluorescence intensity of BNCDs@Tb-MOF@DNAzyme with an increase in concentration of Pb2+ ions, (b), (c), & (d), Stern-Volmer plot representing I0/I versus Pb2+ concentration in an aqueous solution at 450 nm, 490 nm, and 544 nm, respectively.
Specificity of the sensing probe toward Pb2+ ions. Here, the relative photoluminescence taken at three consecutive peaks 450, 490, & 544 nm of BNCDs@Tb-MOF@GR5 is shown for different metal ions.

Fluorescent sensing of Pb2+ ions in real water samples: (a) gradual elevation in fluorescence intensity of BNCDs@Tb-MOF@DNAzyme with an increase in concentration of Pb2+ ions in spiked real water samples from different locality.
Limits of detection (LODs) for recently reported MOF sensors for Pb2+.
Determination of Pb2+ ions in different real water samples.

head.ce:title: Carbon quantum dots@metal–organic framework based catalytic nucleic acid fluorescent system for highly sensitive and selective detection of Pb2+ in aqueous solutions

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: Accumulation of heavy metal ions in the ecosystem has become a major concern due to their adverse impacts on animals, humans, and aquatic habitats. The development of a rapid and portable sensing system is important for the prompt detection and proper management of such pollutants. Herein, we have designed a fluorescence-based biosensor for the selective and sensitive detection of Pb2+ ions using a DNAzyme system that is active only in the presence of lead (II) ions (Pb2+). The developed nanoprobe relies on two major components: (i) boron and nitrogen carbon dots (BNCDs)-doped carboxyl functionalized-terbium metal-organic framework (COOH-Tb MOF) as a fluorescent tag and (ii) quencher-modified catalytic NH3-GR5 DNAzyme as a bioreceptor molecule. The fluorescent tag (BNCDs/MOF) exhibits dual fluorescence with emission peaks located in the regions of blue and green. In the absence of Pb2+ ions, this hybridization undergoes reduced emission intensity because of the overlap in fluorescence emission between BNCDs@Tb-MOF and quenchers. However, as the catalytic core of the DNAzyme strand is activated in the presence of Pb2+ ions, the fluorescent emission of BNCDs/Tb-MOF resumes with the cleavage of quencher-tagged substrate strand. Here, the release of a shorter oligo sequence effectively increases the intensity of the proposed novel biosensor to realize low level detection of Pb2+ (e.g., to 0.96 ppb in a wide detection range from 2 to 1000 nM). The establishment of this novel biosensing approach is expected to offer new insights into rapid, selective, and sensitive detection of major pollutants for food and environmental safety.

ce:data-availability.ce:para: Data will be made available on request.

ce:section.ce:para: Contamination of water resources has become a major concern in the global environment. Water contaminants can be classified as chemical (such as heavy metals, pesticides, bleach, and salts) and microbiological contaminants (such as parasites, bacteria, and viruses). Among them, heavy metals are the most hazardous components due to their toxicity, persistent nature, and cumulative effects. The ingestion of heavy metals through contaminated water has an adverse impact on human health in various respects (e.g., mutations, displacement of essential metal ions (such as zinc and magnesium) from metalloproteins, and cancer) [1,2]. Among different heavy metal ions, lead (Pb) is among the most frequently detected heavy metals in water due to corrosion of service pipes, effluents from industries, and electronic-waste recycling activities [3,4]. To protect humans from potential exposure, efforts have been made to set standards and guidelines to limit the presence of heavy metals in potable water (e.g., those by the United States (US) Environmental Protection Agency (EPA) and world health organization (WHO)). For Pb2+ ions, the maximum level of contamination in water was set to 10 ppb (WHO) or 15 ppb (EPA) [5,6]. Despite strict regulations, the early and rapid detection of lead in water is desirable to properly prevent short- or long-term exposures. Among many sensors developed for the detection of heavy metal ions, colorimetric and fluorescent sensing mechanisms lead the end user market in terms of methodological superiority, low cost, ease of use, speed, and on-site detection [7–11]. However, the practicality and efficacy of fluorescent biosensors can be limited if applied for sensing of specific heavy metal pollutants (such as Pb2+ ions) in complex real-world media. Therefore, there is a need for further development of Pb2+ fluorescent sensors that are user friendly and capable of prolonged operation.

In recent years, fluorescent oligonucleotide-based probes have been reported as sensitive and simple detection methods [12]. Artificial catalytically-active DNA molecules (DNAzymes) are widely used as molecular sensing tools for biosensing events due to their versatile design, quick reaction kinetics, and high stability together with excellent specificity in recognizing the analyte [13,14]. These more sensitive molecular beacons have been applied for in situ detection and quantification of a variety of metal ions [15–17]. However, these DNAzyme-based fluorescent sensors suffer from inherent defects of organic dye fluorophores (e.g., broad emission properties and photobleaching) that can limit their performance in terms of sensitivity and stability. To combat these limitations of conventional fluorophores, several fluorescent nanomaterials have been developed such as quantum dots (QDs) with high fluorescence intensity and photostability [18]. Alam et al. [19] reported the controlled hydrothermal synthesis of chlorophyll-functionalized CQDs for fluorescent detection of As3+ and Hg+ ions in water. This turn-on/off sensor offers strong binding of As3+ to carbonyl groups of chlorophyll moieties and weak binding of Hg+ ions to carbon atoms of the QDs. The combination of CQDs with other QDs has also been reported for multiplex determination of metal ions [20]. However, QDs in aqueous solutions can agglomerate, which limits the performance of the fluorescent sensor via a non-radiative deactivation process. Moreover, QDs also suffer from thermal/air instability and quenching in a sensing medium [21]. The encapsulation of QDs into carboxymethyl cellulose microspheres has been reported to prevent their aggregation and also to offer good chemical properties [22]. The encapsulation/interfacing of QDs with other luminescent nanomaterials (like metal–organic frameworks; MOFs) can improve their performance in terms of photostability, background fluorescence, and signal to noise ratio [23]. These nano-frameworks either alone or as a conjugated system have been successfully employed as the fluorescence material for sensing of different analytes including heavy metal ions [24,25]. In particular, Taher et al. [26] used amine-functionalized Cd-BDC MOF for the detection of mercury in a competitive aqueous medium having other metal ions. Zhang et al. [27] reported the fluorescent detection of chromate ions in water using a sulfur QDs-doped UiO-66-NH2 composite. Further, a zeolitic imidazolate framework (ZIF)-67 encapsulated with polyethylene glycol (PEG)-capped ZnS QDs has been reported as a host–guest hybrid system for fluorescent detection of Cu (II) ions in water [28].

In an effort to develop an ideal fluorescent sensing platform with high selectivity, the present work deals with a hybrid combination of dual emissive CDs-MOF with a lead-specific DNAzyme. Herein, we took Breaker and Joyce Pb2+-dependent DNAzyme (GR-5) [29] as a model sensing compound to improve the sensitivity and selectivity of a QDs@MOF-based fluorescent biosensor. Highly luminescent boron-nitrogen doped carbon dots (BNCDs) and terbium metal organic framework (BNCDs/Tb-MOF) conjugate was fabricated as a dual-emissive fluorescent label with quencher-modified GR-5. The Pb2+ dependent cleaving activity of the DNAzyme diminished the interaction between fluorescent BNCDs/Tb-MOF and quencher. This cleavage activity should cause the generation of a luminescent response via a fluorescence resonance energy transfer (FRET) approach. The significant recovery of fluorescence due to such cleavage activity imparts ultrasensitive detection of Pb2+ ions in aqueous solutions. To address above discussed concerns and project an efficient, acceptable and affordable solution, this research, for the first time, employs lanthanide MOF in conjunction with carbon dots as a signal transduction element. High-intensity fluorophores require less excitation light and hence cause less photobleaching. Moreover, the stability of the fluoroprobe is also needed for time-lapse imaging. Therefore, GR5 DNAzyme as a specific probe through the integration of highly luminescent materials and suitable quenchers offers both specific and efficient detection of lead ions. This DNAzyme-based fluorescent biosensor demonstrates noticeable benefits (e.g., specificity, selectivity, and high sensitivity) over traditional sensors for metal iondetection. In addition, a dual-labelled recognition probe was designed to have extremelylow background fluorescence through inter- and intra-strand quenching effects as a feasible solution for enhancing sensitivity against diverse environmental pollutants.

The quencher-labelled sequences of the GR-5 (1) Pb-Enz (5′-ACA GAC ATC ATC TCT GAA GTA GCG CCG CCG TAT AGT GAG-3′-BHQ1) and (2) Pb-Sub (NH2-(CH2)6-5′-CTC ACT AT rA GGA AGA GAT GAT GTC TGT −3′- Dabcyl) oligonucleotides used in the present work [30] were synthesized and (HPLC) purified by Aritech Chemazone Private Limited, Kurukshetra, Haryana (India). Dual quenchers Dabcyl and BHQ1 were preferred for labelling of oligonucleotide sequences at the 3′-end of the substrate and catalytic strand, respectively. Citric acid, ethylenediamine (EDA), boric acid, trimesic acid, tetraethyl orthosilicate (TEOS), 4-(2-hydroxyethyl)piperazine-1-ethanesulfonic acid (HEPES) sodium salt, 2-(N-morpholino)ethanesulfonic acid (MES) sodium salt, and glutaraldehyde were purchased from Sigma-Aldrich. Terbium(III) nitrate pentahydrate (Tb(NO3)3·5H2O), 4,4′-trimethylenedipyridine and other reagents were obtained from Alfa Aesar. All analytical grade reagents were used as acquired without any further purification. Nanosep spin filters were purchased from Merck. Ultrapure water with an electrical resistivity of 18.2 M × cm was employed for all the experiments.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Apparatus utilized for synthesis and characterization
ce:section.ce:para: The synthesized CDs, MOF, and CD-MOF-DNAzyme conjugates were characterized by different spectroscopy and microscopy techniques. Their hydrodynamic diameters, polydispersity indexes (PDIs), and zeta potentials were analyzed using a Zetasizer Nano ZS90 (Malvern). UV–vis spectra were recorded on a Shimadzu UV-3600 spectrometer. The purity and concentration of the obtained single stranded oligonucleotide was quantified by measuring the absorbance at 260 nm and 280 nm using a UV–vis spectrophotometer (Shimadzu BioSpec nano). Fourier transform infrared (FTIR) spectroscopy was undertaken on a Perkin Elmer spectrophotometer ranging from 400 to 4,000 cm−1. X-ray diffraction (XRD) patterns of synthesized materials and their conjugates were measured on a PANalytical X’Pert-Pro diffraction device (0.02°/sec) where a rotating anode X-ray generator (working at 40 kV and 40 mA with Cu Kα radiation) was employed. X-ray photoelectron spectroscopy (XPS) analysis was carried out using a Thermo ESCALAB 250Xi X-ray photoelectron spectrometer (Thermo). Field emission scanning electron microscopy (FESEM) was used to investigate the topographical and morphological features of the synthesized materials using a JEOL JSM- 7610FPlus microscope. Energy dispersive X-ray spectroscopy (EDS) analysis data were acquired using an AMETEK energy-dispersive X-ray (EDX) system integrated with FESEM with a resolution of 127 eV. High resolution-transmission electron microscopy (HR-TEM) along with selected area electron diffraction (SAED) was utilized to explore the detailed structures of materials using an FEI Tecnai G2 S-Twin transmission electron microscope. The accuracy and practical efficacy of the proposed nanobiosensor, in terms of measurement of minimum concentration Pb2+,is analysed with inductively coupled plasma-mass spectrometry (ICP-MS: Agilent 7700 series).

Synthesis for BNCDs and Tb-MOF are in the Supporting Information [refer to Section 1.1 and 1.2 in the Supporting Information (SI)].The BNCD/Tb-MOF conjugate was prepared using an in situ hydrothermal approach. Briefly, 0.3 g of Tb(NO3)3·5H2O (30 mmol) was dissolved in 10 ml of a BNCD solution. Two other types of solutions were prepared by dissolving 0.15 g of 4,4′-trimethylenedipyridine (TMDPy) (25 mmol) and 0.157 g trimesic acid (BTC) (0.40 mmol) in 10 ml of H2O and ethanol, respectively. After that, all these solutions were mixed and further transferred into a 100 ml steel autoclave sealed at 150 °C for 72 hrs. The precipitates obtained through centrifugation of the autoclaved sample were washed three times by distilled water and ethanol (alternating) for elimination of unreacted ingredients. Eventually, BNCD/Tb-MOF was dried at room temperature.

To achieve sensitive fluorescent measurement of Pb ions, the surface of the pre-synthesized BNCD/Tb-MOF was silanized using TEOS to address the environmental quenching effect. TEOS was diluted with an equal volume of ethanol [31]. Later, 1 ml of BNCD/Tb-MOF was added to the TEOS solution, and it was heated to 60 °C for 120 min. Further, hydrolysis of TEOS was carried out by drop-wise addition of ammonium hydroxide that allows the uniform coating of SiO2 on a BNCD/Tb-MOF surface. The silanized BNCD/Tb-MOF solution was purified through repeated washes alternating between distilled water and ethanol, and it was finally stored at 4 °C.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Preparation of GR-5 DNAzyme sequence-based signal probe labelled by BNCDs/Tb-MOF
ce:section.ce:para: The purified single stranded 100 μM oligonucleotide stock solutions were prepared in ultrapure nuclease free water, and then a master stock solution was frozen at −80 °C. Initially, salinized BNCDs/Tb-MOF (2 mg ml−1) was dispersed in 5 ml of 2.5 wt% glutaraldehyde (0.01 M PBS buffer at pH 7.4) through stirring for 5 hrs at 28 °C [32]. Unreacted glutaraldehyde was removed through PBS washing followed by suspension in filtered 0.01 M PBS. Then, an activated BNCD/Tb-MOF sample was mixed with the Pb-specific oligo (100 μL, 2 μM) strand (refer to section 1.3 in the SI) and was incubated overnight at 4 °C. The unconjugated aptamer sequence was removed through Nanosep filters through centrifugation at 10,000 g for 5 min. The BNCD/Tb-MOF labelled with GR5 substrate was incubated with catalytic GR5 DNAzyme strand to obtain active double stranded (ds)-DNAzyme. The two oligo strands were added together in equal molar amounts to avoid residual single stranded material in HEPES buffer (pH 7.2). Thereafter, the reaction mixture was heated to 95 °C for 10 min in a heat block and were cooled gradually. The final BNCD/Tb-MOF@GR5 DNAzyme bioconjugates were stored at 4 °C for further experiments and analysis.

The fluorescence spectra of all synthesized materials (i.e., BNCDs, Tb-MOF, BNCDs/Tb-MOF, and BNCDs/Tb-MOF@GR5 DNAzyme) were obtained on a Spectra max M2, molecular device spectrofluorometer using a black fluorescence microplate. The optical properties of synthesized materials were recorded in suspension form at a 250 nm excitation wavelength with a slit width of 3 nm. The emission spectrum was collected within the range of 250–800 nm. Fluorescent sensitivity of designed biosensor towards Pb2+ was examined in a concentration range from 0 nM to 1000 nM in 50 mM HEPES buffer solution (pH = 7.0). The addition of Pb2+ into the BNCD/Tb-MOF@ GR5 probe solution was made independently at concentrations of 2, 7, 15, 25, 50, 100, 250, 500, and 1000 nM. The binding of Pb2+ on the active site of the GR5 DNAzyme induces catalytic cleavage of the antisense substrate strand to release 5́BNCDs/Tb-MOF fragments. The release of fluorescent tagged fragments resulted in fluorescence peaks primarily at 450, 490 and 544 nm. To evaluate the selectivity and specificity of developed biosensor for Pb2+, an interference assay was performed against the possible interfering metal ions such as Ni2+, Cu2+, Cd2+, Mn2+, Zn2+, Co2+, Na+, and Li+ in a similar manner at a concentration of 100 μM for all. Real water samples were analysed using fabricated nanobiosensing “BNCD/Tb-MOF@ GR5” probe in order to evaluate its practical applicability for detection of Pb(II). The real water samples were collected from the different locations e.g. handpump (Hisar), Blue Bird Lake water (Hisar), tap water (Chandigarh), NABI laboratory tap water (Mohali, Panjab), tap water (Manoli, Mohali). Collected real water samples were enriched with a 2, 5, 25, 250 and 500 nm Pb2+ metal ions solution and tested with standard ICP-MS detection method along with fluorescent probe, to verify the accuracy and reliability of the fabricated system.

In comparison to other Pb2+-based DNAzymes (like 17E which are active with other divalent metal ions), GR-5 specifically reacts only with Pb2+ ions [33]. In GR-5 DNAzyme, a 15-nucleotide base pair (bp) oligo strand (which is hybridized to chimeric antisense substrate strand) contains an rA cleavage site downstream of the “GG” sequence [34]. The toxic Pb2+ ions occupy the active binding site to stimulate the catalytic core of the enzymatic GR5 strand. This interaction leads to the cleavage of the phosphodiester bond of internal rA of the substrate strand. The GR-5 DNAzyme also exhibits enzyme kinetics that obey well-known Michaelis Menten equations with a catalytic rate constant (kcat) = 1 min−1 and Michaelis constant Km = 2 µM. The cleavage of substrate in the presence of GR-5 DNAzyme is about 105 fold faster than that of the spontaneous uncatalyzed reactions [35]. The designed DNAzyme-based fluorescent sensor consists of dual luminescent carbon dot-conjugated Tb-MOF and quencher-labeled GR-5 DNAzyme and its corresponding substrate [35]. Further, the substrate at its 5′ end was modified with a nanomaterial-based fluorescent label (BNCD/Tb-MOF) via glutaraldehyde-based covalent crosslinking to condense two amine groups. In the absence of Pb2+ metal ions, the DNAzyme and substrate complex were hybridized to each other so that the fluorescent emission of BNCD/Tb-MOF came near the absorption range of the BHQ molecules. Therefore, the dual (blue and green) fluorescence of BNCD/Tb-MOF was efficiently quenched by the BHQ molecules via the FRET process in the range of 480–580 nm (with maximum absorption at 534 nm). In the presence of Pb2+ metal ions, the GR-5 enzyme causes cleavage of the substrate strand to measure Pb2+ by restoring the fluorescence of QD/MOF.

Two single stranded oligos (GR5-S and GR5-E) with complementary sequences were employed for the sensing of Pb2+ ions. The 2 oligo strands were added together in equal molar amounts to prepare the ds-GR5-DNAzyme. This step is critical to avoid the residual single-stranded material. First, the mixed oligonucleotides were heated to 94 °C for 5 min in a heat block and were then allowed to gradually cool down. The resulting DNAzyme in a stable double-stranded form was stored at 4 °C. The hybridization of oligos was confirmed by ethidium bromide (EtBr) and fluorescent dye stained in 1.5 % agarose gel. Ethidium bromide was used as an easy and sensitive stain for DNA along with low background interferences and strong intercalation with ds-DNA oligos. It offered a detection limit of 1–5 ng/band. This insensitive staining of ssDNA or RNA requires a relatively large amount of nucleic acid (∼10-fold) for significant detection. Success of the annealing reaction was checked on 1.5 % agarose gel. The mobility of single-stranded oligos differs from the markers of the same length due to double strands of DNA ladders. In Fig. 1 (a), the intense band corresponding to ds-DNA is visible in lane 2, while no visible bands were observed in lanes 3 loaded with single stranded (ss) oligos. This was further confirmed using agarose gel stained with safe Diamond™ Nucleic Acid fluorescent Dye sensitive to both ss-DNA, and ds-DNA. Analysis of annealed fragments is shown in Fig. 1 (b). Here, DNA bands in lanes 3 and 4 contain ss-GR5-S and GR5-E. The migration of dsDNA in lane 2 is slower than ether ss-oligos.

Initially, the structural information and size information of synthesized BNCDs were examined by HRTEM as seen in Fig. 2 (a). BNCDs possess a spherical structure with an average diameter of 7 nm. The SAED pattern of BNCD with well-defined diffraction rings along with a blurry cloud indicates the presence of both amorphous and crystalline biocompatible CDs (Fig. 2b). The d spacing between lattice planes of BNCDs was calculated to be 1.51 nm through live profiling of IFFT images (Fig. 2 (c-d)). Further, the morphological characteristics of Tb-MOF and BNCDs-doped Tb-MOF were evaluated through FESEM, as depicted in Fig. 3 (a-c). The microscopic structure information of these materials was also investigated by HRTEM (Fig. 3(d-f)). Fig. 3(a) shows that Tb-MOF particles form aggregates from the radial arrangement of rod-shaped structures, while higher magnification of rods showed a layered structure (inset of Fig. 3(a)). The fine rod shape MOFs and their aggregate forms have average widths of about 27 ± 5 and 1910 ± 100 nm, respectively. After interacting with BNCDs, Tb-MOF and its silanized form exhibited similar structures as presented in Fig. 3(b) and (c), respectively. The aggregates of rod-shaped structures of the composite confirmed the negligible influence of QD doping on the morphology of Tb-MOF. The spherical BNCDs fit well between the pores of the Tb-MOF (refer to BET analysis, section 1.4 in the SI). The BNCDs@Tb-MOF and silanized-BNCD@Tb-MOF rods showed average width values of 22.6 ± 5 nm and 23.2 ± 5 nm, respectively (Fig. 3b & c inset). The higher magnification TEM images confirmed that BNCDs were successfully embedded in the Tb-MOF (Fig. 3 e). The silanization (i.e., covering of BNCD/TbMOF with a few layers of silicon nanoparticles) is also shown in Fig. 3 (e). The crystallinity of Tb-MOF and its composite was also confirmed through SAED profiling (Fig. 3 (g - i)).
The EDS spectra of BNCD, Tb-MOF and its composites to analyse the concomitant presence of different elements are shown in Fig. S4-S6 (Details in section 1.5 in the SI).

The presence of different functional groups in synthesized materials was confirmed by FTIR analysis (Fig. 4 (a): black line). In the case of BNCDs, the absorption band at 1456 cm−1 was attributed to B-O stretching vibrations that confirmed the interaction of boric acid with the surface of CDs [36]. The broad stretching vibration centered at 3226 cm−1 was assigned to the ν(OH) and NH bonds. In addition to boric acid functionalization, the characteristic peaks at 1653 and 1334 cm−1, which correspond to CO symmetrical and C-OH stretching vibrations respectively, should ensure the carboxylation of the BNCDs [37]. Therefore, hydrophilic functionalities embedded in BNCDs facilitate their easy dispersion into an aqueous solution and covalent conjugation with Tb-MOF. In the case of Tb-MOF FTIR spectrum (red line), the characteristic band observed at 3400.07 cm−1 indicates OH groups due to the presence of coordinated water. The absorption peak at 1875 cm−1 represents a weak combination-overtone band representing –COO- in benzene rings [38]. Further, the peaks at 1614 and 1556 cm−1 correspond to the stretching vibrations of CC and CN, respectively, due to the presence of bipyridine. All other absorption peaks at 1372, 1112, and 533 cm−1 may conform to CH or = CH vibrations of the benzene ring [38]. Further, the FTIR spectrum of BNCDs@Tb-MOF conjugates (blue line) resemble those of both BNCD and Tb-MOF with a slight deviation in some characteristic peaks (1718, 1402, and 1243 cm−1) possibly due to the significant conjugation of BNCDs and Tb-MOF [39]. The crystallinity of all synthesized materials was confirmed by XRD patterns (Fig. 4b) [40,41]. The XRD diffractogram of Tb-MOF and BNCDs@Tb-MOF composites confirmed the P crystallinity of the triclinic space group (Details in section 1.6 and Table S1 in the SI)[42].

ce:section.ce:section-title: Photoluminescence and UV absorption spectroscopic analysis
ce:section.ce:para: The optical properties of BNCDs and Tb-MOF along with their hybrid BNCDs@Tb-MOF have significant implications in biosensing applications (Fig. 8 ). The UV spectrum of BNCDs manifests the presence of two absorption peaks at 235 and 355 nm corresponding to the π − π*or n- π* transitions of aromatic unsaturated sp2 domains (CC group) (Fig. 8 (a)) [3]. In the PL spectrum of QDs, the most intense emission occurs at 448 nm with an excitation around 300 nm. These hydrophilic BNCDs exhibit a modest excitation-dependent emission that represents the changes in the position of the emission spectrum (blue to orange) with variations in excitation wavelength in the range of 250 to 424 nm (Fig. 8(b)) [41]. The synthesized BNCDs exhibit a broad emission with a full width at half-maximum (FWHM) of 74 nm (color purity of 66.5 %). The quantum yield of CDs can be influenced by a number of factors or processes (e.g., functionalization, coating agent, and the dispersion medium) [45]. Here, BNCDs showed a very high quantum yield of 60.21 % in the aqueous phase (section 1.7 in the SI). Further, Tb-MOF displayed a UV–vis absorption peak around 260 nm (Fig. 8d), while BNCDs@Tb-MOF possesses a strong peak at about 270 nm with a shoulder peak at around 220 nm (Fig. 8g) (refer to section 1.8 in the SI for band gap calculations). A BNCDs/Tb-MOF aqueous dispersion offers a stronger greenish blue luminescence (inset of Fig. 8g) under UV illumination (λ = 365 nm) than the green emission from the bare Tb-MOF (inset of Fig. 8d). In the PL spectrum of Tb-MOF, there are four characteristic bands of terbium including those at 490 nm (blue), 544 nm (green), 583 (yellow), and 620 nm (orange) upon excitation at 250 nm (Fig. 8e). All these peaks correspond to specific transitions like 5D4 - 7F6, 5D4 - 7F5, 5D4 - 7F4, and 5D4 - 7F3, respectively [40]. The quantum yield of Tb-MOF was 43 % in the aqueous phase. The designed Tb-MOF exhibited fine emission with a FWHM = 3.8 nm (λ = 544 nm) with a color purity of 42.5 %. The BNCDs/Tb-MOF conjugated nanoprobe exhibits emission peaks from BNCDs at 450 nm along with four distinct elemental emission peaks from Tb3+ at 490, 544, 583 nm, and 620 nm, respectively (Fig. 8 (h)). Apparently, the dual emission from BNCDs/TB-MOF confirmed the successful encapsulation of BNCDs into the cavities of Tb-MOFs. The optical spectrum of the nanocomposite (Fig. 8 (i)) covers nearly all the visible range from 400 to 680 nm. That is, blue to pink with a maximum in the green region. FWHM of the nanocomposite was calculated to be 13.4 nm with a color purity of 25.9 %. The optical spectra of all these materials (i.e., BNCDs, Tb-MOF, and BNCDs@Tb-MOF) are shown in Fig. 8(c, f, & i), respectively. Here, BNCDs showed a broad PL response ranging from 400 to 600 nm. Tb-MOF showed sharp emission peaks at distinct wavelengths. Likewise, the composite covered nearly all the visible range from 400 to 680 nm (i.e., blue to pink) with a maximum in the green region. Fluorescence stability of BNCDs@Tb-MOF nano-conjugate was stable in water for up to 60 days (see section 1.9 in the SI).

Chromaticity diagrams were also plotted for all synthesized materials to calculate the CIE 1931 chromaticity co-ordinates with PL excitation at 250 nm (Fig. 9 ). In the case of BNCDs, these coordinates (0.1852, 0.1682) symbolize the purplish-blue shade of emission, as seen in Fig. 9(a). For Tb-MOF, CIL coordinates (0.3058, 0.5434) represent the bluish green shade of emission (Fig. 9 b) in comparison to the greenish blue shade of emission for the composite with (0.2495, 0.3863) chromaticity co-ordinates (Fig. 9 (c)).

ce:section.ce:section-title: Characterization of fabricated BNCDs@Tb-MOF@DNAzyme sensing probe
ce:section.ce:para: In the presence of Pb2+, this classic DNAzyme cleaves an RNA base embedded in the DNA substrates. A Pb2+-coordinated hydroxyl group facilitates the deprotonation of the 2′ hydroxyl, which is located near the cleavage site [29]. Specifically, the purines (adenine and guanine) and pyrimidines (cytosine) coordinate with Pb2+ at the N7 position and N3 position. Moreover, Pb2+ can also coordinate with the O6 and O4 position of guanosine and uracil, respectively. It is capable of speeding up chemical transformations by following the Michaelis-Menten kinetics. The reaction was set according to the single turnover kinetics where a high concentration of DNAzyme was preferred in comparison to the substrate [46]. For optimal performance of the biosensing probe, the 1:2 strand ratio of the GR5-Dabcyl S strand to the BHQ1-GR-E strand was preferred [47]. The DNAzyme complexes were prepared by incubating the mixture at 95℃ for 5 min. It was then gradually cooled down to room temperature over 45 min. The fluorogenic active DNAzyme complex was incubated for 20 min to ensure the catalytic cleavage reaction in the presence of Pb2+ [33] (section 1.10 in the SI). The formation of BNCDs@Tb-MOF@GR5 DNAzyme conjugates was confirmed using different methods such as agarose gel electrophoresis (Fig. 10 a), FTIR measurements (Fig. 4a), and DLS measurements (refer to sections 1.11 and 1.12 in the SI). In Fig. 10a, Lane 1 and Lane 2 of agarose gel electrophoresis show the bands for single stranded substrate (s) and DNAzyme (D), respectively. Lanes 3, 5, and 8 show double stranded active DNAzyme, BNCDs@Tb-MOF@GR5, and bare BNCDs@Tb-MOF, respectively. It is apparent that the electrophoretic mobility of the BNCDs@Tb-MOF@DNAzyme conjugate (lane 5) is slower than that of the GR5 DNAzyme (lane 3) alone. However, it is faster than the bare BNCDs@Tb-MOF (lane 8) due to reduced aggregation after attachment with the negatively charged DNAzyme beacon. The DNAzyme conjugate with fluorescent label runs slower due to its larger size. In the FTIR spectrum, the chemical conjugation of DNAzyme to BNCDs@Tb-MOF is likely to induce additional absorption peaks at 1070, and 1282, 1376 cm−1. These peaks can be attributed to the stretching vibrations of PO4 3- and bases in oligos (refer to Fig. 4a; pink color) [48]. The conjugation of the GR5-DNAzyme with BNCDs@Tb-MOF results in the formation of catalytically active fluorescent probes. As shown in Fig. 10 (b), the red curve shows the formation of the active BNCDs@Tb-MOF@substrate structure as it quenches the fluorescence of BNCDs@Tb-MOF, e.g., 20.78, 28.90 and 30.60 % quenching efficiencies against 450, 490, and 544 nm wavelengths, respectively. Note that little quenching of blue and green fluorescence occurs due to intramolecular FRET between 5′ BNCDs@Tb-MOF and 3′ Dabcyl of ss-GR5 substrate molecule. To further improve the quenching efficiency of the sensing nanoprobe, the GR5 substrate was hybridized to antisense DNAzyme tagged to another quencher BHQ1. The hybridization of catalytically inactive GR5-E strand to the BNCDs@Tb-MOF functionalized GR5-S strand further quenched the fluorescence with 35.37 %, 84.24 %, and 96.70 % efficiency at 450, 490, and 544 nm wavelengths, respectively.

To evaluate the true sensing performance of the fabricated probe, the influence of the dilution buffer (in terms of Na2+ concentration, Mg2+ concentration, pH, and incubation time) has also been done (for details, refer to section 1.13 and Fig S12 in the SI). The results of this study are in consistent with the literature (Breaker and Joyce, 1994). Therefore, the buffer conditions for the subsequent experiments are preferred as 50 mM NaHEPES, 50 mM NaCl and 5 mM MgCl2 at pH 7.26 in order to ensure stable enzyme–substrate complex.

To investigate the feasibility of using a DNAzyme biosensor for the detection of Pb2+ ions, the activation of DNAzyme was examined against 1000 nM Pb2+ ions. This activation causes the separation of 5′ fragment the GR5-S strand to help restore the fluorescence of the BNCDs@Tb-MOF. The significant change in the fluorescence of this biosensing probe can be represented as “ON” and “OFF” states in the presence and absence of Pb2+-ions, respectively. The fluorescent intensity after GR5-E strand-catalyzed cleavage of MOF-QD/GR5-S strandcan be used to quantify the concentration of Pb2+. To determine the sensitivity of this biosensor, the variation in fluorescence intensity of BNCD@Tb-MOF@DNAzyme (0.1 mg ml− 1) was studied with increasing concentration of Pb2+ from 0 to 1000 nM. This increase in Pb2+ concentration eventually led to a gradually elevated fluorescence signal at 450 nm (due to BNCDs), 490 nm, and 544 nm (due to Tb-MOF) [Fig. 11 a].

The relationship between the Pb2+ concentration and the luminescence enhancement of synthesized nano-bioprobe was further analyzed using the Stern–Volmer curve (Fig. 11b, c, and d). The Stern–Volmer curve shows a good linear relationship along with Stern-Volmer constant (Ksv) values of 1.4 × 10-9 M−1, 1.1 × 10-9 M−1, and 4.5 × 10-9 M−1 for Pb2+ for the three consecutive peaks at 450 nm, 490 nm, and 544 nm, respectively. The fabricated biosensing probe offered limits of quantification (LOQs) of 2.99 ppb with a low limit of detection (LOD) of 0.96 ppb at 450 nm in accord with the previously reported MOFs sensors for Pb2+ (Table 1 ). The reported literature on lead sensors suffers from different limitations either in terms of specificity and selectivity or sensitivity. The synthesized BNCDs/Tb-MOF composite resolves the issue of low absorptivity and quantum yield of conventional fluorophores, whereas the use of Pb2+-specific DNAzyme improved the specificity and selectivity of the biosensor.

To examine the specificity of the BNCDs@Tb-MOF@GR5 DNAzyme toward Pb2+, the fluorescence of the sensing probe was examined for a wide array of similar metal ions including Ni2+, Cu2+, Cd2+, Mn2+, Zn2+, Co2+, Ca2+, Na+, and Li+ (ref. to Fig. 12 ). The developed sensor shows a fluorescence response specifically towards Pb2+, irrespective of the presence of other metal ions due to enhanced specificity of DNAzyme. This study confirmed the significant potential of BNCDs@Tb-MOF@DNAzyme probes as remarkably selective sensors for Pb2+ detection. The fabricated sensing electrode is highly attractive and includes high sensitivity, wide detection range (0–100 nm), naked eye detection, and dual fluorescence mode. Moreover, the impact of interfering factors (such as internal background fluorescence due to reagents and environment) was found to be negligible or minimal. Interestingly, the limit of detection (0.96 ppb) of the fluorescent biosensing probe was also lower than the maximum permissible level of Pb (15 ppb) in drinking water, as recommended by US EPA [5].

ce:section.ce:section-title: Analysis of Pb2+ ion in real water sample
ce:section.ce:para: The performance of developed biosensing system was also evaluated for the real water samples and spiked samples. Real water samples taken from different sources showed no obvious fluorescent signals. This was due to the absence of Pb2+ concentration, as confirmed by ICP-MS results. In case of spiked real water samples, the developed biosensing platform showed sharp fluorescent peaks, Fig. 13 . The spiked real water samples were also tested with ICP-MS to get the concentration of Pb2+ ions, Table 2 . The recovery percentage in the range 92–119.0 % demonstrates that the fabricated probe has an excellent capability for accurate determination of Pb(II) in real water samples. Moreover, the relative standard deviation (RSD) were in a range of 0.4–1.7 lg/L, indicating that the BNCDs@Tb-MOF@DNAzyme has significant potential for practical applicability for the determination of Pb2+ ions in real samples.

In this work, we designed a novel fluorescent nano-biosensor (BNCD/TbMOF@GR5 DNAzyme) with dual blue-green emission. Water soluble BNCDs were encapsulated in highly luminescent Tb-MOF via an in situ hydrothermal approach. The fluorescent BNCDs@MOFs composite showed excellent optical properties of both BNCDs and Tb3+. Moreover, the nano-framework conjugates also exhibited good structural and fluorescence stability in an aqueous solution. The established fluorescence sensor showed a low LOD of 5.97 nM over a wide linear range (0–1000 nM). Furthermore, the impacts of both internal (e.g., background fluorescence) and external factors (e.g., pH and time) were negligible or minimal. As a result, we recommend adopting this biosensor as a smaller and low-cost device for the rapid detection of lead in real water samples. This biosensing approach should be able to build a general and effective strategy to construct bi-functional MOF-based fluorescence sensors for highly sensitive detection in the environment under low-resource settings.
